retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Pathophysiology -- Pulpitis and Pulp Necrosis
retrieved_docs.content: The pulp is the center part of a tooth directly beneath the dentin layer, which contains nerves and blood vessels. Bacterial decay can erode through the enamel and dentin layers and enter the pulp; this causes an infection of this tissue, resulting in pulpitis. The microbes causing pulpitis continue to be an area of study, but it is predominantly thought that anaerobic bacteria are the leading cause of pulp infections. [35] Pulp necrosis results when the pulpitis infection progresses and causes irreversible damage and cell death.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- History and Physical -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with reversible pulpitis usually present with a severe toothache exacerbated by temperature changes; as the pulp vitality further deteriorates into irreversible pulpitis and necrosis, dental pain becomes more constant. As pulp necrosis develops, the tooth becomes discolored and can appear yellow, gray, brown, or black.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Etiology -- Pulp Involvement
retrieved_docs.content: Pulpitis is the inflammation of the dental pulp, which can be caused by bacterial by-products reaching the pulp from dental caries, bacteria entering the pulp through the apical foramen from a periodontal infection, or through a tooth fracture. [5] Furthermore, traumatic and chemical insults can also result in pulpal inflammation. [6] The early stage of pulpitis elicits mild, intermittent dental pain, typically triggered by thermal stimuli, eg, cold drinks, but without spontaneous pain. This is known as reversible pulpitis, and treatment involves the removal of the carious tissue and the placement of a restoration.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Pulpitis requires treatment for pulp protection and insertion of restorative materials if considered reversible. Either root canal therapy or tooth extraction treats irreversible pulpitis and necrosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Severe inflammatory reactions can lead to pulpal cell injury, potentially compromising pulpal vitality and survivability. Although the infection activates an immune response, immune cells and molecules are unable to effectively penetrate dentin to remove pathogens due to the narrow apical foramen of the tooth and the hard enamel surrounding the internal structures. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Etiology -- Pulp Involvement
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics do not play a role in the management of irreversible pulpitis, as clinical signs of infection, eg, swelling of the adjacent mucosa, typically do not accompany this condition. [7] Current evidence provides limited support for the effectiveness of antibiotics in reducing dental pain or decreasing the need for analgesics in patients with untreated teeth affected by irreversible pulpitis. [8] Consequently, antimicrobial therapy is not initiated before referral for definitive dental treatment. Over time, irreversible pulpitis may progress to ischemia and necrosis of the dental pulp, often leaving the affected tooth asymptomatic despite underlying pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Once pulpal vascularity is dysfunctional, progression to pulpal necrosis and apical pathosis is typical. [2] Pulpal necrosis is the "death of cells or tissues through injury or disease, especially in a localized area of the body." [5] Pulpal necrosis is a continuum, as partial necrosis can exist before complete necrosis occurs.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Etiology -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: The progression of dental infections can lead to deep space infections of the head and neck and osteomyelitis of the facial bones. In 1 study, dental infections were the most common cause of almost 50% of deep neck abscesses. [12] Likewise, dental infections were found to be the etiology for over 90% of patients who presented with Ludwig angina, a rapidly progressive and sometimes fatal infection of the soft tissues of the mouth and neck, which can ultimately cause airway compromise. [13] Thus, early identification and prompt treatment of dental infections are important to prevent more severe, widespread, and life-threatening infections.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Introduction -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Almost all dental infections begin with dental caries. Dental caries is highly prevalent - experienced by nearly 100% of adults worldwide. [4] The oral cavity is a polymicrobial environment, and extravasation of the bacteria into previously sterile oral spaces is the source of different types of dental infections, which include pulpitis, pulp necrosis, periodontitis, periapical and periodontal abscesses, necrotizing periodontal disease, and pericoronitis. Proper oral hygiene, mitigation of health risk factors, and early identification and treatment reduce the prevalence of dental infections and the risk of life-threatening complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: A principal cause of microbial flare-ups is the apical extrusion of pulpal debris. [23] When debris is extruded through over-instrumentation and incorrect working lengths, the host's response increases inflammation to combat the extruded organisms. This acute inflammatory reaction increases vascular permeability, leading to edema. With increased hydrostatic pressure, nerve endings become compressed and generate pain. In addition, incomplete chemo-mechanical canal preparation during non-surgical endodontic therapy can cause remaining bacteria to multiply and express virulence factors, resulting in further damage to the periapical tissues. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: There are two variations of pulpitis: reversible and irreversible. Reversible pulpitis is characterized by mild to moderate inflammation that recedes with the management of the cause of injury. Frequent causes of reversible pulpitis include bacterial infection from caries, acute trauma, repetitive trauma from bruxism, thermal shock, excessive dehydration of a cavity during restoration, and even irritation of exposed dentin. Unlike reversible pulpitis, irreversible pulpitis has only one cause, specifically, compromise of the pulp by bacterial infection beyond the point of no return where healing is not possible. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Necrotizing Periodontal Diseases -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Early identification of clinical signs is essential to prevent progression. A history of necrotizing periodontal disease is associated with an increased risk of recurrence, necessitating regular follow-up. Timely dental intervention at symptom onset improves outcomes and reduces the risk of complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Initial Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: The early stage of apical periodontitis is an inflammatory process where neutrophils are extravasated to the area through chemotaxis. The neutrophils attack and kill the offending microorganisms and release leukotrienes and prostaglandins, which attract more neutrophils and macrophages to the site. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Pathophysiology -- Periodontitis and Necrotizing Periodontal Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontal disease progresses from gingivitis and involves transition and accumulation from aerobic to anaerobic bacteria such as Aggregatibacter actinomycemcomitans and Porphyromonas gingivalis. This fosters a host inflammatory response that further contributes to the condition. While periodontal disease is not considered a dental emergency, it becomes emergent when it progresses into a necrotizing infection. While the pathogenesis of necrotizing periodontal disease is not entirely understood, it is largely thought that a reduced host immune response allows commensal oral organisms to become pathogenic. These bacteria produce several metabolites that subsequently cause the rapid destruction of the periodontium. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Pathophysiology -- Periapical Abscess
retrieved_docs.content: The apex of a tooth is the area where the nerve enters the tooth through the root tip. Bacterial infections of the pulp can progress and collect in the periapical region, creating an abscess. Initially, this abscess is small and focal to the involved tooth. However, local extension of this infection can lead to serious complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Prognosis -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, studies have shown that teeth and periodontal tissue that experience repeated infections or have preexisting periodontal disease carry a worse prognosis. [70] [71] The prognosis is generally improved in situations where risk factors and comorbidities (such as tobacco use, uncontrolled diabetes, and immunological diseases) are addressed and managed appropriately.

In the broader context, التهاب كبيبات الكلى is clinically associated with قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic renal failure), indicating that patients with this type of glomerular inflammation are at risk for progressive loss of kidney function. Recognizing the relationship between necrotizing glomerulonephritis, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, and the potential development of chronic renal failure is essential for timely diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 698
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is repeatedly identified as the primary anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies. Inflammatory disorders localized to the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, metastatic disseminated retinitis‑retinochoroiditis, as well as combined entities such as التهاب المشيمية (choroiditis), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (choroiditis‑retinitis), and the unspecified focal variant التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد. Post‑inflammatory and post‑traumatic sequelae—post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar—are also situated in the choroidal tissue. Degenerative and structural changes affecting the choroidea comprise تنكس حصوي (calcific degeneration), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal degeneration), and choroidal sclerosis around the optic disc in both partial (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) and total (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) forms. Vascular complications recorded as choroidal findings include نزيف مشيمي باثق (persistent choroidal hemorrhage), hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي), and non‑hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين). The choroidea is additionally noted to have a relationship with بُقَعُ روث, although the clinical significance of this association is not detailed. Moreover, the choroidea is described in Arabic as “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating that pathological observations may be localized to a specific genetic chorionic region. Recognizing the choroid as the common locus for these inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, degenerative, vascular, and post‑injury scarring processes—and for its genetic and ancillary associations—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted imaging, and appropriate therapeutic planning in ophthalmic practice.
